ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper is an incremental rather than radical novelty: it builds on the existing “compare–aggregate” paradigm (citing Parikh et al. 2016, Wang & Jiang 2016b, He & Lin 2016) rather than proposing a wholly new framework. Its main contributions are empirical — applying a compare–aggregate variant (CNN aggregation, one‑directional attention) across four diverse datasets and systematically evaluating six word‑level comparison functions, highlighting element‑wise SUB/MULT as effective. These analyses and cross‑task validation are useful and differentiate the work from prior single‑task studies, but the techniques themselves are modest extensions of known methods (element‑wise ops were previously used by Mou et al. 2016), so novelty is moderate. | B(3): The paper does not introduce a fundamentally new paradigm—it builds on the existing “compare-aggregate” family (e.g. Parikh et al. 2016, Wang & Jiang 2016b, He & Lin 2016)—but it provides a systematic empirical study and some modest architectural tweaks. Its main novel contributions are (1) applying a single compare-aggregate instantiation across four different sequence-matching tasks and (2) a focused, empirical comparison of six word-level comparison functions (notably element-wise SUB/MULT and their combination), showing element-wise ops often work best. These are useful and informative to practitioners but amount to a moderate, primarily empirical/engineering contribution rather than a highly original methodological advance.

Its main contributions are empirical — applying a compare–aggregate variant (CNN aggregation, one‑directional attention) across four diverse datasets and systematically evaluating six word‑level comparison functions, highlighting element‑wise SUB/MULT as effective. These analyses and cross‑task validation are useful and differentiate the work from prior single‑task studies, but the techniques themselves are modest extensions of known methods (element‑wise ops were previously used by Mou et al. 2016), so novelty is moderate.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper does not introduce a fundamentally new paradigm—it builds on the existing “compare-aggregate” family (e.g. Parikh et al. 2016, Wang & Jiang 2016b, He & Lin 2016)—but it provides a systematic empirical study and some modest architectural tweaks. Its main novel contributions are (1) applying a single compare-aggregate instantiation across four different sequence-matching tasks and (2) a focused, empirical comparison of six word-level comparison functions (notably element-wise SUB/MULT and their combination), showing element-wise ops often work best. These are useful and informative to practitioners but amount to a moderate, primarily empirical/engineering contribution rather than a highly original methodological advance.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims (Section 1) that a compare-aggregate framework generalizes across four datasets and that element-wise comparison functions (SUB, MULT, SUBMULT+NN) perform well, and supports them with empirical results reported over MovieQA, InsuranceQA, WikiQA and SNLI (Sections 3.1–3.3). The model equations (preprocessing Eqn.1, attention Eqn.2, comparison functions Eqs.3–8 and aggregation Eqn.9) are standard and appear mathematically consistent. Methodologically there are minor weaknesses: baselines were taken from reported numbers rather than re-implemented (Section 3.2), embeddings were kept fixed with OOVs set to zero (Section 3.3), and no statistical significance testing is reported, which leaves some risk of dataset/setting mismatch. Overall the experimental evidence plausibly supports the claims, with only these minor methodological concerns. | B(4): The paper clearly states its main claims (a general compare-aggregate architecture is effective across diverse sequence-matching tasks, and element-wise comparison functions like SUB/MULT perform very well) and supports them with experiments on four datasets and an ablation discussion (Sections 3, 3.3–3.4). The mathematical parts are straightforward and consistent (attention, comparison functions), with only minor notation ambiguity in Eq. (1) where the element-wise gate operator is implied but not explicitly printed. Experimental methodology is reasonable (multiple public datasets, a range of baselines, and systematic comparison of six comparison functions), but has weaknesses: baselines are taken from reported numbers rather than reimplemented under identical conditions, hyperparameter/architecture choices are not fully harmonized across baselines, and no statistical significance tests are reported. These are methodological issues that warrant caution but do not overturn the empirical support for the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims (Section 1) that a compare-aggregate framework generalizes across four datasets and that element-wise comparison functions (SUB, MULT, SUBMULT+NN) perform well, and supports them with empirical results reported over MovieQA, InsuranceQA, WikiQA and SNLI (Sections 3.1–3.3). The model equations (preprocessing Eqn.1, attention Eqn.2, comparison functions Eqs.3–8 and aggregation Eqn.9) are standard and appear mathematically consistent. Methodologically there are minor weaknesses: baselines were taken from reported numbers rather than re-implemented (Section 3.2), embeddings were kept fixed with OOVs set to zero (Section 3.3), and no statistical significance testing is reported, which leaves some risk of dataset/setting mismatch. Overall the experimental evidence plausibly supports the claims, with only these minor methodological concerns.

Experimental methodology is reasonable (multiple public datasets, a range of baselines, and systematic comparison of six comparison functions), but has weaknesses: baselines are taken from reported numbers rather than reimplemented under identical conditions, hyperparameter/architecture choices are not fully harmonized across baselines, and no statistical significance tests are reported. These are methodological issues that warrant caution but do not overturn the empirical support for the main conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear structure (Introduction → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and explicitly states its two main contributions early in the Introduction. Notation is defined and used consistently in Section 2 (e.g., Q ∈ R^{d×Q}, equations (1)–(9) and the various comparison functions), and the method/experiment flow is easy to follow. However, the provided text contains duplicated/paraphrased passages and a few typographical/formatting glitches (e.g., "long short-term memorynetworks", repeated paragraphs, some garbled symbols) and several figures/tables are referenced (Figure 1, Table 1/2/3, Figure 2) but not present here, which slightly hampers assessment of the visual presentation. Overall the writing is clear with minor presentation issues that warrant cleanup. | B(3): The paper is overall well organized (Intro → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and states the main contributions early (twofold contributions in the Introduction). Notation is mostly consistent and the method section clearly defines Q, A, aj, hj, tj and the six comparison functions. However, the provided text contains many duplicated/garbled sentences, a few minor notational confusions (e.g. reuse of Q/A vs Q/A after preprocessing) and references to figures/tables that are not present in the excerpt, which reduces readability and makes some parts harder to follow.
